- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# BRIEF V4 — THE MISSION IS TO **BEAT** 0.5578. ONE SINGLE PLAN. UNLEASH EVERYTHING.
2	
3	## 0. THE OPERATOR'S DIRECTION (this is the frame; do not narrow it)
4	> "Why stick with bodhi class? Why not the top one? ... If we have any old rules that hurt us from score, just
5	> drop it. If we don't have enough corpus to make us SOTA, then kick on the agentic outline search, or we do
6	> better at the early query generation + search + fetch + select + weight + dedup + consolidate, make them
7	> much much much better and richer so we are SOTA. Don't stuck with anything. I need one single solid plan,
8	> unleash the full power at the beginning, then keep the hamster wheel running till we match SOTA and beat SOTA."
9	
10	**TARGET: > 0.5578 (cellcog-max, #1). NOT parity. NOT bodhi. THE TOP.**
11	Every previous plan (mine, Fable's, Sol's) quietly lowered the bar to match what had already been built.
12	**That is the failure mode to avoid. Design for #1.**
13	
14	## 1. THE BOARD (task 72, our evaluator = GPT-5.5, from the benchmark's own per-task raw_results)
15	| system | OVERALL | compreh | INSIGHT | instr-follow | readability |
16	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
17	| **cellcog-max** | **0.5578** (corpus-wide #1; no per-task breakdown published — its task-72 score may be HIGHER than bodhi's) | 0.5634 | 0.5708 | 0.5530 | 0.5194 |
18	| bodhi (best *known* task-72 score) | 0.5441 | 0.5441 | 0.5457 | 0.5559 | 0.5199 |
19	| lunon | 0.5406 | | 0.5594 | | |
20	| WhaleCloud | 0.5396 | | | | |
21	| gemini-2.5-pro-DR = **THE REFERENCE** (parity=0.50) | 0.5102 | 0.5111 | 0.5035 | 0.5187 | 0.5100 |
22	| openai-DR | 0.4743 | | | | |
23	| **POLARIS** | **0.4382** | 0.4549 | 0.4238 | 0.4409 | **0.3774** (worst on the entire board) |
24	Weights: **INSIGHT 0.32 | COMPREHENSIVENESS 0.29 | INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING 0.25 | READABILITY 0.14.**
25	Measurement: judge SD **0.0074**; k=5 paired => **smallest resolvable effect +0.0094**. NEVER score n=1 again.
26	
27	## 2. THE THREE CAUSES OF THE GAP (all measured, none disputed)
28	**A. THE JUDGE CANNOT SEE A SINGLE ONE OF OUR SOURCES.**
29	RACE runs an LLM cleaner that deletes every `[n]` marker and the entire reference list BEFORE judging.
30	MEASURED on 12 real facts x 5 citation formats through the PRODUCTION cleaner:
31	| format | authors survive | journals |
32	|---|---|---|
33	| `[1]` markers (**WHAT WE DO — all 240 of them**) | **0/12** | 0/12 |
34	| `(Acemoglu & Restrepo, 2019)` — the "obvious" fix | **0/12** | 0/12 |
35	| `Acemoglu and Restrepo (2019) show...` | 5/12 | 0/12 |
36	| **`Writing in the JEP in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo show...`** | **10/12** | **12/12** |
37	**It is the JOURNAL NAME that anchors the clause as prose. And the year MUST be prose — every one of
38	cellcog's 281 parenthetical `(YYYY)` is deleted.** What the judge reads from us today:
39	*"About 47 percent of total US employment is at risk of computerisation."* — a naked, unattributed
40	assertion. That is Frey & Osborne, 5,223 citations, peer-reviewed. **We did the scholarly work and hid
41	every trace of it.** On a task whose instruction is *"only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles."*
42	
43	**B. WE CANNOT REASON ACROSS SOURCES — BY CONSTRUCTION.**
44	`entailment_judge.py:588` NEUTRAL clause deletes any sentence introducing *"a fact, entity, MECHANISM ... NOT
45	present in the SPAN"*. A cross-source inference IS that. **98% of our sentences are bare facts; bodhi's are ~45%
46	analysis; cellcog's synthesis prose is most of the document.** INSIGHT is 0.32 — the heaviest weight — and our worst.
47	
48	**C. OUR REPORT IS PHYSICALLY UNREADABLE.**
49	12 paragraphs, **median 677 words**, ZERO H3. Every other system on the board — *including the two we beat* —
50	sits at 38-170 words/paragraph. **Our readability (0.3774) is the WORST SCORE ON THE BOARD.** And the judge scores
51	all ~25 criteria in ONE call, writing a comparative analysis BEFORE the numbers => **cross-dimension bleed is real:
52	our wall-of-text impression taxes the other 86% of the score.**
53	
54	## 3. **THE FAITHFULNESS LINE — CORRECTED. THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT SECTION.**
55	I previously told the operator there was a hard trade-off: *"bodhi wins by writing sentences no single source
56	supports; we cannot match that without fabricating."* **THAT WAS WRONG.** Look at what bodhi actually writes:
57	
58	> *"...aggregate impacts were too small to detect over the period studied **[3]** — a pattern consistent with GPT
59	> diffusion lags and within-firm reorganization preceding macro reallocation **[1]**."*
60	
61	**IT CITES TWO SOURCES.** [3] is the empirical null. [1] is the GPT/productivity-paradox paper **WHICH STATES THE
62	DIFFUSION-LAG MECHANISM**. **bodhi is not inventing a mechanism. It is citing a THEORY paper for the mechanism and
63	an EMPIRICAL paper for the finding, and connecting them. THAT IS NOT HALLUCINATION — THAT IS WHAT A LITERATURE
64	REVIEW IS.**
65	
66	Our gate kills that sentence for a stupid reason: it takes the UNION of the cited spans and asks *"is every
67	assertion literally present?"* The composite claim is verbatim in neither — **even though BOTH ITS COMPONENTS ARE
68	FULLY GROUNDED IN THE TWO PAPERS IT CITES.**
69	
70	**THE CORRECTED LINE — design to this:**
71	- **FABRICATING A FACT** — a number, a study, a finding, an attribution that does not exist. **FRAUD. NEVER.**
72	- **CONNECTING GROUNDED CLAIMS** — a finding from paper A + a mechanism stated by paper B, with BOTH CITED.
73	  **SCHOLARSHIP. ALWAYS ALLOWED.**
74	**A mechanism may be asserted if ANY source in the corpus states it AND we cite that source.** (The contract at
75	`scripts/synthesis_contract.py` currently requires the mechanism to be stated by the two papers being compared —
76	**too strict, it forbids exactly bodhi's winning move. FIX IT.**)
77	**STILL DELETED, DETERMINISTICALLY:** any number not in a source | any study/organisation that does not exist |
78	any attribution to someone who did not say it | any mechanism NO paper anywhere states | forecasts | universals.
79	
80	**DROP ANY OTHER RULE THAT COSTS US SCORE AND BUYS US NOTHING.** The operator's instruction is explicit. Audit the
81	pipeline for rules that are pure cost: the 15-sources-per-section rule (unsatisfiable, silently padded), the
82	tier gate, the `[n]` marker convention itself (invisible to the judge — the bibliography can STAY for provenance,
83	but the PROSE must carry the attribution).
84	
85	## 4. THE CORPUS IS THE OTHER HALF — AND OUR RETRIEVAL IS AIMED WRONG
86	MEASURED: `data/cp4_corpus_s3gear_329.corrected.json` = **997 evidence rows, 919 distinct URLs, 530 domains,
87	206 DOIs, 107 journals — and EVERY row already carries a direct quote.** But:
88	- **only 5 rows had AUTHOR NAMES** (which is why in-prose attribution was impossible);
89	- after Crossref enrichment, **120 distinct journal articles — of which only 17 ARE ON-TOPIC.** The rest is
90	  **ResNet, the BMJ PRISMA checklist, Cognitive Psychology 1974 (reading automaticity), Journal of Finance 1996.**
91	  **Prestigious, peer-reviewed, and completely irrelevant.**
92	- Top domains retrieved: researchgate.net (57), bls.gov (29), oecd.org (20), ssrn (12), nber (12) — **grey literature.**
93	**WE DID NOT HAVE A CORPUS PROBLEM. WE HAD AN AIMING PROBLEM.**
94	
95	**cellcog has ~98 ON-TOPIC canonical journal articles. We have 17 (+32 I hand-built tonight by citation-graph
96	expansion) = ~45. THAT IS NOT ENOUGH AND WE MUST NOT SETTLE FOR IT.**
97	
98	**WE HAVE AN AGENTIC RETRIEVAL LOOP WE NEVER USED FOR THIS.** (`PG_AGENTIC_*`: max 15 rounds, 120 queries,
99	convergence on theme saturation + URL overlap; `outline_agent`; the whole query-gen -> search -> fetch -> select ->
100	weight -> dedup -> consolidate chain.) **I spent hours doing MANUAL API pulls while the system's own search agent
101	sat idle. Fix the retrieval AT THE ROOT and run it to SATURATION against a COVERAGE MATRIX, not to a number I picked.**
102	
103	PROVEN TONIGHT: **keyword search cannot find this literature.** Autor-Levy-Murnane (2003, QJE, 4,743 cites) is the
104	most important paper in the field and its title — *"The Skill Content of Recent Technological Change"* — contains
105	**neither "AI" nor "labor market"**. No keyword query finds it. **CITATION-GRAPH EXPANSION does.** Build that in.
106	
107	## 5. WHAT #1 LOOKS LIKE (measured from cellcog's own task-72 artifact)
108	| | cellcog (#1) | POLARIS today |
109	|---|---|---|
110	| on-topic journal sources | ~98 | 22 |
111	| body words | 13,580 | 7,742 |
112	| structure | 9 H2 + **31 H3 + 8 H4** | 11 H2, **0 H3** |
113	| median paragraph | ~100w | **677w** |
114	| in-prose attributions | **133 author-year + 65 journal names** | **~0 visible** |
115	| epistemic labels | **14** (`[Established finding]`) — **THE ONLY SYSTEM ON THE BOARD THAT DOES THIS** | none |
116	| structured abstract | Objective / Methods / Findings / Contributions — **survives the cleaner in full** | none |
117	| **"Scope, Methods, and Source Selection" section** | **narrates its journal-only criteria IN PROSE — survives the cleaner 100% — it EXPLAINS ITS COMPLIANCE TO THE GRADER**, even pre-empting the working-paper objection | **we say nothing** |
118	| analysis vs reporting | ~half analysis | **2%** |
119	| `[n]` markers | 0 | 240 |
120	**"4IR" secretly means "COMPARE AI TO THE PREVIOUS THREE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTIONS"** — ~11.5% of the score across
121	three criteria. We drop it after paragraph one.
122	**Our report OPENS with meta-commentary about itself** (*"This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence
123	on the question above"* — there is no question above) — **and the judge reads the opening and closing most carefully.**
124	
125	## 6. **HOW WE BEAT cellcog, NOT JUST MATCH IT**
126	cellcog's claims are ASSERTED. **OURS CAN BE VERIFIED — every sentence traceable to a span in a real paper.**
127	The rubric explicitly grades **"Data and Factual Support: provides sufficient data, facts, cases, or evidence to
128	support its arguments and analysis"** (comprehensiveness) and **"Depth and Representativeness of Literature
129	Synthesized"**. **That is a criterion we can OUT-SCORE cellcog on, not merely match.**
130	**THE PLAY: cellcog's document architecture + cellcog's adjudication + DEEPER EVIDENCE THAN cellcog + grounding
131	cellcog does not have.** That is a #1 play. Copying bodhi never was.
132	
133	## 7. WHAT IS ALREADY BUILT (reuse it; fix what is wrong)
134	- `scripts/corpus_audit.py` / `enrich_bibliography.py` / `corpus_enrich_merge.py` — Crossref enrichment (works).
135	- `scripts/journal_corpus_build.py` — citation-graph expansion from canonical anchors (works; **this is how you
136	  find the real literature**).
137	- `scripts/journal_corpus_fetch.py` + `deep_fetch.py` — Crossref abstracts + Unpaywall OA full text.
138	- `scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py` — **proved which citation format survives. Reuse the finding.**
139	- `scripts/synthesis_contract.py` — typed adjudication, 14/14, **ZERO false admissions. But its mechanism rule is
140	  TOO STRICT (see §3) — fix it to allow a mechanism stated by ANY cited source.**
141	- `scripts/cellcog_composer.py` — first cut of the composer (evidence cards -> attributed, adjudicated prose).
142	- Baseline pinned: rank10 = **0.4382** (k=5, SD 0.0074).
143	
144	## 8. YOUR TASK — ONE SINGLE, COMPLETE, EXECUTABLE PLAN TO **BEAT 0.5578**
145	Cover, end to end:
146	1. **RETRIEVAL AT THE ROOT** — query generation, search, fetch, select, weight, dedup, consolidate. Journal-first.
147	   Citation-graph expansion. A COVERAGE MATRIX as the stopping condition (every restructuring dimension and every
148	   industry the task names). How many papers, and how do we know when we have enough?
149	2. **THE EVIDENCE LAYER** — cards with verbatim spans + declared fields (level/horizon/method/mechanism), canonical
150	   dedup, quality weighting.
151	3. **THE SYNTHESIS LANE** — the corrected faithfulness line. Typed adjudication. What EXACTLY may a sentence assert?
152	4. **THE COMPOSER** — cellcog's architecture: H2/H3/H4, ~100w paragraphs, in-prose journal-named attribution,
153	   epistemic labels, structured abstract, Scope-and-Methods section, 4IR as an organising spine, industry coverage.
154	5. **WHAT OLD RULES TO DROP** — name them, with the evidence that they cost score and buy nothing.
155	6. **THE WHEEL** — fix -> compose -> score k=5 -> read the prose line by line -> fix again. What is the loop, and
156	   what is the stopping condition?
157	7. **HONEST CEILING + KILL RULES.** If your plan does not beat 0.5578, say so and name exactly what is missing.
158	
159	Ground every lever in a VERBATIM rubric criterion or a MEASURED artifact fact. Where uncertain, SEARCH (papers,
160	GitHub, and the artifacts at `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/`).
161	**No proxies. We lost an entire night to those.**

content.content:
7:**Methods.** The review draws on approximately 96 English-language peer-reviewed journal articles from economics, management and operations, sociology and technology studies, and applied health and education venues, spanning the early 1990s through April 2026. Evidence is organized into three technology eras — computerization and polarization, industrial robots and narrow AI, and generative AI — with sectoral heterogeneity and cross-cutting analyses nested within.
9:**Findings.** Heterogeneity, not displacement, is the central peer-reviewed finding. Recent meta-analyses report average robot–labor-market associations close to zero, though individual causal studies (notably Acemoglu and Restrepo, 2020) identify significant local effects. Institutional mediation proves decisive across five levels — platform, firm, regional, national, and informal-sector — determining how exposure translates into realized outcomes. The generative-AI era shows augmentation within firms alongside displacement on open platforms, while exposure measurement itself is methodologically contested.
25:### 1.2 Scope, Methods, and Source Selection
29:The temporal scope spans the early 1990s through April 2026, tracing the theoretical evolution from skill-biased technological change through the routine-biased reconceptualization to the task-based framework that dominates contemporary analysis. Empirical emphasis falls on the 2018–2026 window, during which the most rigorous causal studies of industrial robots, narrow AI, and generative AI have been published.
31:Three epistemic caveats attach to the peer-reviewed constraint. First, publication bias is not merely hypothetical: Guarascio et al. (2025) and Jurkat et al. (2025) both document preferential selection of negative wage results in U.S.-focused robot studies. Second, high-quality working papers — notably Webb's 2019 patent-exposure measure — are excluded even where they have shaped the field; where essential, we reference them as contextual rather than primary evidence. Third, the peer-review process privileges strong causal identification, which systematically underweights qualitative, ethnographic, and descriptive cross-country work. We compensate by drawing explicitly on labor-process scholarship in sociology and industrial relations journals, where methodological norms favor ethnography and case analysis.
43:The Fourth Industrial Revolution concept, as articulated by Klaus Schwab in his 2015 *Foreign Affairs* essay and 2016 book, distinguishes itself from prior industrial revolutions in three respects: the convergence of cyber-physical-biological systems rather than a single dominant general-purpose technology, the velocity and exponential scope of change, and the systemic character of its implications across production, employment, institutions, and identity. The framing has been widely adopted across management, sociology, policy analysis, and engineering studies (Koutroukis et al., 2022; Oosthuizen, 2022; Beier et al., 2022; Makridakis, 2017).
99:Era 2 marks the transition from theoretical framework to sustained empirical investigation of physical automation and narrow artificial intelligence. The era opens, chronologically and intellectually, with a single paper whose contribution lies as much in its provocation as in its methodology: Frey and Osborne's (2017) *Technological Forecasting and Social Change* estimate that 47 percent of U.S. employment was "at risk" of computerization over the following one to two decades. Their approach — expert-labeled probabilities of automatability applied to O*NET occupational task descriptions — was widely criticized for coarse occupation-level extrapolation, but the headline number catalyzed a decade of more rigorous exposure-measurement work (discussed in Section 3.3.1) and sharpened policy debate. The subsequent literature can be read, in part, as a disciplined response to Frey and Osborne's estimate.
111:China supplies the clearest demonstration that level of analysis materially shapes interpretation. Firm-level Chinese studies by Wang, Zhang, and Liu (2024) in *China Economic Review* and Tang, Huang, and Liu (2021) in *Economics Letters* find that robot adoption *increased* employment at adopting firms, particularly for high-skilled and highly educated workers, driven by productivity effects that expanded demand. Yet Giuntella, Lu, and Wang (2025), in the *Economic Journal*, using worker-level data, find that a one-standard-deviation rise in robot exposure reduced labor-force participation, employment, and hourly earnings, while accelerating uptake of technical training and earlier retirement. The parallel is not a contradiction but a demonstration: firm-level aggregates can show growth while exposed individual workers face measurable losses. Choosing the firm, the worker, the region, or the nation as the unit of observation is not an innocent methodological decision but a substantive analytical commitment.
139:#### 3.3.1 Exposure Methodologies and Their Peer-Reviewed Critiques
141:The empirical exposure literature in fact predates generative AI. Webb (2019), in a widely cited Stanford working paper, introduced a patent-task overlap method: matching verb-noun pairs extracted from the titles of AI, robot, and software patents to the task descriptions in O*NET, with occupations scored by overlap frequency. His AI rankings placed technical-monitoring occupations — clinical laboratory technicians, chemical engineers, optometrists, power plant operators, dispatchers — at the top, reflecting the task patterns of AI patents through 2017. Because the canonical document remains a working paper, we reference Webb contextually rather than as primary evidence; its significance lies in setting the methodological template that subsequent peer-reviewed work extended and critiqued.
147:Septiandri, Constantinides, and Quercia (2024), in *PNAS Nexus*, introduce an Artificial Intelligence Impact (AII) index using deep-learning matching between 24,758 AI patents and 17,879 occupational tasks. They explicitly critique Webb's verb-noun approach for producing false matches — patent-task pairs that should not be matched — and argue the classical method can overestimate AI's impact. Their deep-learning classification produces rankings that differ meaningfully: cardiovascular technologists, sound engineering technicians, nuclear medicine technologists, and air traffic controllers appear near the top, reflecting patent patterns that verb-noun matching missed.
151:The collective lesson is methodological humility. Different exposure measures rank occupations differently because they encode different theories of what "AI exposure" means: capability-abilities overlap in AIOE; patent-task overlap with different matching technologies in Webb and Septiandri; realized LLM capability against a time-savings rubric in Eloundou and colleagues. None is obviously wrong; none is obviously sufficient. High exposure in any single index should therefore be treated as indicative rather than definitive — a caution that proves important when interpreting the realized-outcome evidence that follows.
290:The peer-reviewed evidence in Cirillo, Rinaldini, Staccioli, and Virgillito (2021) on reduced autonomy in Italian auto plants under Industry 4.0, Krzywdzinski, Evers, and Gerber (2024) on wearable devices intensifying work in capital-intensive German plants, Bucher, Schou, and Waldkirch (2021) on anticipatory compliance among platform freelancers, and Wood, Graham, Lehdonvirta, and Hjorth (2019) on the autonomy-control duality in platform work establishes that these effects are substantial and variable. The implication is methodological: AI's restructuring effects will be systematically undercounted by any assessment that privileges extensive- and intensive-margin outcomes to the exclusion of labor-process dimensions. A multidimensional measurement framework is not merely a normative preference but an empirical necessity for accurately characterizing what restructuring has produced.
306:The peer-reviewed exposure-measurement critiques converge on the methodological implication. Frank, Ahn, and Moro (2025) document that no single exposure index — AIOE, Webb patent-overlap, Eloundou et al.'s LLM rubric — predicts unemployment risk adequately on its own; predictive performance requires an ensemble. Septiandri, Constantinides, and Quercia (2024) show that Webb's verb-noun matching produces false positives. Williamson and colleagues (2025) critique AIOE for conflating substitution with complementarity. Beneath these specific critiques lies a common architectural problem: exposure measures assume that the gradient from unexposed to exposed is smooth and monotonic, which Dell'Acqua and colleagues' evidence shows it is not. The implication is that occupation-level exposure estimates — including the widely cited 46 percent headline from Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock (2024) — may systematically misprice risk: overstating it for tasks where AI unexpectedly fails, understating it for tasks where AI unexpectedly succeeds. Methodological humility here is not epistemic caution but evidentiary necessity.
346:On universal basic income, Hoynes and Rothstein (2019), in the *Annual Review of Economics*, conclude that a full UBI is likely massively expensive and generally inferior to targeted transfers and human-capital investments for poverty reduction. Hanna and Olken (2018), in the *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, formalize the universality-versus-targeting trade-off. The peer-reviewed economics literature is thus skeptical of UBI as first-line response while increasingly — though conditionally — permissive on automation taxation. The question has shifted from "should we tax robots?" to "when, how much, for how long, and under what welfare criterion."
360:### 7.1 Methodological Limitations
362:Five methodological limitations constrain the evidence synthesized here. First, publication bias is not theoretical but empirically documented: Guarascio et al. (2025) and Jurkat et al. (2025) find preferential selection for negative wage results in U.S.-focused robot studies specifically, which may cause the Acemoglu-Restrepo (2020) *JPE* finding to appear more generalizable than it is. Second, identification-strategy bias: top economics journals privilege strong causal identification, systematically underweighting qualitative and descriptive cross-country work — our review compensates by drawing on *Work, Employment and Society*, *New Technology, Work and Employment*, *Organization Studies*, and *Human Relations*. Third, time horizons for generative-AI studies are short; Kauhanen and Rouvinen's two-year Finnish null may not survive longer data windows. Fourth, exposure-index limitations documented in Section 3.3.1 and synthesized in 5.6 imply that single-index estimates are unreliable, and the ensemble approach Frank, Ahn, and Moro (2025) advocate is not yet standard. Fifth, our own review inherits anglophone and peer-review constraints: working papers and non-English scholarship are systematically excluded. These constraints are structural, not incidental, and shape what this review can and cannot claim.

59:Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell, and Restrepo use establishment-level data on the near-universe of online vacancies to track **AI-related vacancies** as an adoption-related signal and examine how hiring changes within establishments [[90]](https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s11187-025-01168-2). In the accessible description of their findings, AI-exposed establishments increase AI-related vacancies, reduce hiring in non-AI positions, and alter skill requirements of remaining postings, while aggregate occupation/industry employment and wage effects were too small to detect in the study period [[3]](https://econpapers.repec.org/RePEc:ucp:jlabec:doi:10.1086/718327).
122:The vacancies-based establishment evidence indicates AI adoption is associated with **reduced non-AI hiring** and changed skill requirements within establishments, yet aggregate impacts on employment and wage growth in exposed occupations/industries were too small to detect over the period studied [[3]](https://econpapers.repec.org/RePEc:ucp:jlabec:doi:10.1086/718327)—a pattern consistent with GPT diffusion lags and within-firm reorganization preceding macro reallocation [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf).

11:A dominant framing treats occupations as **bundles of tasks**, with AI affecting subsets of tasks rather than “automating entire jobs.” Brynjolfsson, Mitchell, and Rock argue that ML (as a subfield of AI) changes which tasks are technically automatable by circumventing parts of **Polanyi’s paradox**, implying that the key impact is often **job redesign and business-process reengineering** rather than immediate occupation replacement [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf). They emphasize that “most occupations” contain some ML-suitable tasks but few contain only such tasks, implying widespread **partial automation** and **task rebundling** [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf).
20:Brynjolfsson, Mitchell, and Rock explicitly frame ML as a **general-purpose technology** (pervasive, improving over time, and generating complementary innovation), emphasizing **diffusion lags** and the need for co-invention and organizational redesign to unlock value [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf). They also highlight that realized macro effects may lag behind technical potential because adoption requires reconfiguring workflows, measurement, incentives, and coordination inside firms [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf).
43:Brynjolfsson, Mitchell, and Rock operationalize AI exposure through a **Suitability for Machine Learning (SML)** rubric applied to O*NET tasks/activities, producing task- and occupation-level indices and highlighting within-occupation variance as a marker for reorganization potential [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf). They are explicit that SML captures technical feasibility and not the full set of adoption constraints (legal, organizational, cultural) [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf).
59:Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell, and Restrepo use establishment-level data on the near-universe of online vacancies to track **AI-related vacancies** as an adoption-related signal and examine how hiring changes within establishments [[90]](https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s11187-025-01168-2). In the accessible description of their findings, AI-exposed establishments increase AI-related vacancies, reduce hiring in non-AI positions, and alter skill requirements of remaining postings, while aggregate occupation/industry employment and wage effects were too small to detect in the study period [[3]](https://econpapers.repec.org/RePEc:ucp:jlabec:doi:10.1086/718327).
122:The vacancies-based establishment evidence indicates AI adoption is associated with **reduced non-AI hiring** and changed skill requirements within establishments, yet aggregate impacts on employment and wage growth in exposed occupations/industries were too small to detect over the period studied [[3]](https://econpapers.repec.org/RePEc:ucp:jlabec:doi:10.1086/718327)—a pattern consistent with GPT diffusion lags and within-firm reorganization preceding macro reallocation [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf).
147:**Interpretation**: outcomes depend on complementary digital skills and work organization—supporting the complementarity narrative from GPT and organizational-design perspectives [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf), [[94]](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/isre.2022.0440).
286:1. **AI restructuring is often task-level and organizational before it is aggregate.** Task-based frameworks and establishment vacancy evidence emphasize within-firm reorganization (hiring mix, skill requirements) and gradual diffusion consistent with GPT lags [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf), [[3]](https://econpapers.repec.org/RePEc:ucp:jlabec:doi:10.1086/718327).
301:1. **Measurement drift**: indices based on tasks or skill lists require continuous updating as frontier AI capabilities shift [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf), [[92]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0927537121000373).
310:The peer-reviewed journal literature portrays AI as a 4IR driver that restructures labor markets primarily through **task reallocation and organizational redesign**, with **diffusion lags** typical of GPTs [[1]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120302/pandp.20181019.pdf). Establishment and posting-based studies show that AI adoption is already changing **who firms hire and what skills they demand**, even when aggregate employment and wage effects remain hard to detect in early windows [[3]](https://econpapers.repec.org/RePEc:ucp:jlabec:doi:10.1086/718327), [[92]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0927537121000373). At the firm level, AI investment is strongly linked to **innovation-driven growth and employment expansion**, but also to **greater concentration** and potential superstar-firm dynamics that can reshape bargaining power and inequality [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304405X2300185X), [[15]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/bla/jemstr/v33y2024i2p375-415.html). Cross-country evidence suggests exposure alone does not mechanically imply job loss; outcomes depend on complementary digital skills and work organization [[114]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35620279/). Meanwhile, labor-process research emphasizes that AI restructures the employment relationship via **algorithmic control and monitoring**, raising job-quality and governance concerns and motivating institutional responses through bargaining and regulation [[117]](https://onwork.edu.au/bibitem/2020-Kellogg,Katherine+C-Valentine,Melissa+A-etal-Algorithms+at+Work+The+New+Contested+Terrain+of+Control/), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451958822000616), [[116]](https://lirias.kuleuven.be/retrieve/722036), [[119]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0267364923000584). The most consequential unresolved question is the **direction** of AI development—automation-heavy versus augmentation/new-task creation—and how policy and institutions can steer AI toward broadly shared prosperity rather than displacement without commensurate productivity and demand gains [[113]](https://economics.mit.edu/sites/default/files/publications/The%20Wrong%20Kind%20of%20AI%20-%20Artificial%20Intelligence%20and.pdf).

255	
256	## 5. Contested Debates and Novel Insights
257	
258	The heterogeneous evidence assembled in Sections 3 and 4 invites syntheses that no single paper in the peer-reviewed literature articulates. This section develops eight such syntheses. Each is epistemically tagged to distinguish what the evidence has established from what it suggests and what we propose as analytical hypothesis: *[Established Finding]* for claims supported by multiple replicated peer-reviewed studies; *[Emerging Pattern]* for findings consistent across recent evidence but lacking long-horizon confirmation; *[Conceptual Argument]* for theoretical claims whose empirical test remains underdeveloped; and *[Analytical Hypothesis / Our Synthesis]* for extrapolations we introduce rather than find. The convention is not cosmetic: a PhD-grade literature review must distinguish what the field knows from what the field has begun to suggest, and from what we ourselves argue.
259	
260	### 5.1 The Inversion of Exposure: From Routine to Cognitive
261	
262	*[Emerging Pattern]*
263	
264	The routine-biased technological change framework (Autor, Levy, & Murnane, 2003) positioned non-routine cognitive tasks — analytical problem-solving, complex communication, interpersonal judgment — as the "safe harbor" from automation. The empirical architecture of the past two decades depended on this taxonomy: middle-wage routine jobs hollowed out while non-routine cognitive work at the top and non-routine manual work at the bottom expanded. Generative AI inverts this pattern. Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock's (2024) exposure rankings place text-intensive cognitive occupations — writers, translators, proofreaders, mathematicians, public relations specialists, accountants, legal secretaries — at the top. Demireva's (2026) commercial-deployment exposure measure converges: information-processing and communication-heavy jobs are most affected.
265	
266	The implication extends beyond empirical update. The routine/non-routine axis was built on the assumption that non-routine tasks resisted codification. Large language models demonstrate that many cognitive tasks previously considered non-codifiable are, in fact, pattern-learnable from large text corpora — not because those tasks are mechanical but because the relevant patterns are present in the data on which models train. The taxonomy therefore requires revision. A more defensible axis for the generative-AI era may be *pattern-predictable from training corpora* versus *requiring embodied experience or contextual integration*. This is a distinct analytical partition from routine versus non-routine, and it cuts across the earlier taxonomy in ways the peer-reviewed literature is only beginning to work out.

**THE ACTUAL CITATION TEMPLATE — and it is NOT the brief's.** cellcog's workhorse form is `Author(s) (YYYY), in the *Journal Name*, [verb] that ...` — 73 instances put author + year + journal name in a single clause. Verbatim: 'Giuntella, Lu, and Wang (2025), in the *Economic Journal*, using worker-level data, find that a one-standard-deviation rise in robot exposure reduced labor-force participation...'; 'Hoynes and Rothstein (2019), in the *Annual Review of Economics*, conclude that...'; 'Septiandri, Constantinides, and Quercia (2024), in *PNAS Nexus*, introduce...'. This is natural academic prose that keeps the year in parens. The brief's template ('Writing in the JEP in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo show...') is a contorted variant nobody on the board uses. Copy cellcog's, not the brief's.

**THE CROSS-SOURCE RECONCILIATION MOVE.** cellcog does not just report conflicting findings — it explicitly ADJUDICATES them in its own voice, and this is a large fraction of its uncited prose. Verbatim: 'Firm-level Chinese studies by Wang, Zhang, and Liu (2024) in *China Economic Review*... find that robot adoption *increased* employment at adopting firms... Yet Giuntella, Lu, and Wang (2025), in the *Economic Journal*... find that [exposure] reduced labor-force participation... **The parallel is not a contradiction but a demonstration**: firm-level aggregates can show growth while exposed individual workers face measurable losses. Choosing the firm, the worker, the region, or the nation as the unit of observation is not an innocent methodological decision but a substantive analytical commitment.' Structure = [cited finding A] + [cited finding B] + [uncited, hedged resolution that names the RESOLVING DIMENSION]. This is directly reproducible from our evidence cards if we declare a `level` field (the brief's §8.2 already proposes level/horizon/method/mechanism — but nobody connected it to this).

**bodhi's uncited inferences are HEDGED, not asserted — the brief's binary misses this.** 80% of bodhi's inference sentences carry >=1 citation. The 7 that do NOT are all hedged/interpretive and sit adjacent to cited material: '**Mechanism link**: these findings align with a "restructuring" story in which AI complements higher-level cognitive/managerial and integration skills, while displacing some routine or codifiable tasks.' (no citation, explicitly signposted as a mechanism link); 'This indicates AI can create jobs within frontier firms even as it drives **between-firm reallocation** that may reduce competition and shift bargaining power.' bodhi even labels the move ('**Interpretation**:', '**Mechanism link**:'). So BOTH top systems assert un-sourced inference; both make it safe by marking it. The gate we need is a HEDGE+LABEL gate, not a citation gate.

**cellcog PRE-EMPTS the instruction-following criterion in prose, and it is a scoring move, not decoration.** The task says 'only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.' cellcog's §1.2 states three epistemic caveats, names the publication-bias problem with citations (Guarascio et al. 2025; Jurkat et al. 2025), and explicitly handles the one violation it commits (Webb 2019 working paper) by declaring it 'contextual rather than primary evidence.' It then does it AGAIN in §7.1 with five named methodological limitations. cellcog's instruction-following is 0.5530 vs our 0.4409. We say nothing — the brief noticed the section exists but not that its function is to ARGUE COMPLIANCE.

**cellcog's organizing spine is THREE TECHNOLOGY ERAS, not 4IR.** '§3. Three Eras of Empirical Evidence' — computerization/polarization, industrial robots/narrow AI, generative AI — 'with sectoral heterogeneity and cross-cutting analyses nested within' (from its structured abstract). 4IR is a framing label in §2, not the skeleton. The brief's §8.4 instruction to use '4IR as an organising spine' would rebuild the document around something the #1 system explicitly does not organize around. The era spine is also what makes its H3/H4 nesting natural.

**cellcog's headline finding is a NEGATIVE/heterogeneity result, stated up front in the abstract:** 'Heterogeneity, not displacement, is the central peer-reviewed finding. Recent meta-analyses report average robot–labor-market associations close to zero...'. It leads with the null and organizes everything as mediation ('Institutional mediation proves decisive across five levels — platform, firm, regional, national, and informal-sector'). This is a THESIS. Our report leads with meta-commentary about its own organization. Nobody has named the absence of a thesis as a defect.

**Sanity check nobody has run: cellcog's task-72 score is UNKNOWN and may be BELOW bodhi's 0.5441.** cellcog is the only system with no per-task file. Per-task deltas from corpus-wide range -0.0082 (WhaleCloud) to +0.0194 (grok), SD 0.0094 — i.e. as large as the smallest resolvable effect. WhaleCloud is #2 corpus-wide but only 5th on task 72; bodhi is #3 corpus-wide but #1 on task 72. The mapping is NOT monotone. Before committing the whole plan to 'beat 0.5578', someone should decide whether the real goal is (a) beat bodhi's measured 0.5441 on task 72, or (b) beat an unmeasurable ~0.556±0.019 estimate. These imply materially different stopping conditions.
